title: Coral Bleaching: A Consequence of Climate Change

lesson.learn: Coral bleaching, a phenomenon associated with rising sea temperatures, poses a significant threat to the world's coral reefs. This process involves corals expelling their algal symbionts, a critical component of their photosynthetic apparatus, resulting in the degradation of their tissues and a loss of color, hence 'bleaching.' The frequency and severity of coral bleaching events have increased due to anthropogenic climate change, leading to widespread damage to coral reef ecosystems.
lesson.simpler: Coral bleaching occurs when corals expel their symbiotic algae, causing them to lose their color. This can be triggered by elevated sea temperatures, leading to the degradation of coral tissue and a decrease in the ecosystem's resilience.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Coral bleaching is a widespread consequence of climate change, resulting in the degradation of coral reefs and the loss of essential ecosystem services.
lesson.deeperDive: According to NASA's Coral Reef Watch Program (2020), coral bleaching events are becoming more frequent and severe, with the 2016 global bleaching event affecting 70% of the world's coral reefs. The Australian Government's Great Barrier Reef Marine Park Authority has reported that the 2016-2017 bleaching event caused an estimated $1.1 billion in economic losses, while the 2020 bleaching event resulted in the death of 30% of the Great Barrier Reef's coral. These events have significant cascading effects on marine life, including fish populations, which can lead to food security issues for millions of people worldwide. Coral reefs also provide critical shoreline protection, reducing the risk of coastal erosion and flooding. The loss of these ecosystems would have significant economic, social, and cultural impacts on coastal communities.
lesson.funFact: Coral reefs cover only 0.1% of the ocean's surface area but provide habitat for 25% of all marine species. This highlights the importance of preserving these ecosystems in the face of climate change.

people: Dr. Mark Eakin - NASA's Coral Reef Watch Program
Dr. Ove Hoegh-Guldberg - University of Queensland, Coral Bleaching Researcher
Dr. Ellen Pikitch - Save the Sound, Ocean Conservationist
years: 1998 - First recorded mass coral bleaching event due to El Niño
2016 - Massive bleaching event affects 70% of global coral reefs
2020 - Third consecutive year of bleaching for the Great Barrier Reef
concepts: Climate Change - Rising sea temperatures cause coral bleaching
Coral-Algal Symbiosis - Corals and algae live together in harmony
Ecosystem Services - Coral reefs provide shoreline protection, fisheries support, and more
Biodiversity Hotspots - Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth
